chapter_name: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Elements

concepts.name: Minerals and Ores
concepts.explanation: Minerals are naturally occurring chemical substances in the earth's crust. Ores are minerals from which a metal can be extracted profitably. Gangue is the unwanted earthy material associated with the ore.

concepts.name: Metallurgy – Overview of Steps
concepts.explanation: Metallurgy involves three major steps: (1) Concentration of the ore (removal of gangue); (2) Isolation of the metal from the concentrated ore (conversion to oxide, then reduction); (3) Purification of the metal (refining).

concepts.name: Concentration of Ores – Hydraulic Washing (Gravity Separation)
concepts.explanation: Based on differences in gravities of the ore and gangue particles. An upward stream of running water washes away the lighter gangue particles, leaving the heavier ore particles behind.

concepts.name: Concentration of Ores – Magnetic Separation
concepts.explanation: Based on differences in magnetic properties. If either the ore or the gangue is magnetic, they can be separated using a magnetic roller. Used for iron ores (e.g., magnetite, haematite).

concepts.name: Concentration of Ores – Froth Flotation Method
concepts.explanation: Used for sulphide ores. The powdered ore is suspended in water with collectors (pine oils, xanthates) and froth stabilisers. Mineral particles become wet by oils and are carried to the froth, while gangue particles remain in water. Depressants (e.g., NaCN) can separate different sulphide ores.

concepts.name: Concentration of Ores – Leaching
concepts.explanation: Leaching involves dissolving the ore in a suitable solvent. Examples: (i) Bauxite (Al₂O₃) is leached with NaOH to form sodium aluminate; (ii) Ag and Au are leached with dilute NaCN or KCN in the presence of air.

concepts.name: Leaching of Alumina from Bauxite (Bayer's Process)
concepts.explanation: Bauxite is digested with concentrated NaOH at 473-523 K and high pressure. Al₂O₃ dissolves as sodium aluminate, while SiO₂, Fe₂O₃, and TiO₂ remain. CO₂ is passed to precipitate hydrated Al₂O₃, which is heated to give pure Al₂O₃.
concepts.formula_reaction: Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH + 3H₂O → 2Na[Al(OH)₄]; 2Na[Al(OH)₄] + CO₂ → Al₂O₃·xH₂O + 2NaHCO₃; Al₂O₃·xH₂O → Al₂O₃ + xH₂O

concepts.name: Calcination
concepts.explanation: Calcination involves heating the ore in the absence of air or in a limited supply of air to remove volatile matter, decompose carbonates, and drive off water. Example: ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂; Fe₂O₃·xH₂O → Fe₂O₃ + xH₂O.
concepts.formula_reaction: ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂; CaCO₃·MgCO₃ → CaO + MgO + 2CO₂

concepts.name: Roasting
concepts.explanation: Roasting involves heating the ore in a regular supply of air at a temperature below the melting point of the metal. Sulphide ores are converted to oxides. SO₂ is produced and used for H₂SO₄ manufacture.

concepts.name: Gibbs Energy and Thermodynamics of Reduction
concepts.explanation: The feasibility of reduction is determined by Gibbs energy (ΔG = ΔH - TΔS). A negative ΔG indicates the reaction will proceed. ΔG is temperature-dependent; increasing T makes the reaction more favourable if ΔS is positive.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS; ΔG° = -RT ln K

concepts.name: Ellingham Diagram – Principles
concepts.explanation: An Ellingham diagram is a plot of ΔG° vs T for the formation of metal oxides. It helps predict the feasibility of thermal reduction. The lower the line, the more stable the oxide. A metal oxide can be reduced by another metal whose oxide line is lower at that temperature.

concepts.name: Ellingham Diagram – Interpretation
concepts.explanation: Each plot is a straight line with a positive slope (except at phase changes). The point where two lines intersect indicates the temperature above which reduction is feasible. The difference in ΔG° values indicates how easily reduction occurs. Limitations: does not account for kinetics.

concepts.name: Reduction of Metal Oxides using Carbon and CO
concepts.explanation: C and CO are common reducing agents. The C→CO line goes downward with temperature, making coke a better reducing agent at high temperatures. CO is used at lower temperatures. The choice depends on the intersection of the oxide line with C/CO lines.
concepts.formula_reaction: MₓO + C → xM + CO; MₓO + CO → xM + CO₂

concepts.name: Extraction of Iron – Blast Furnace
concepts.explanation: Iron oxide ores (haematite, magnetite) are mixed with limestone and coke and fed into a blast furnace. Hot air is blown from the bottom. Reactions occur at different temperature zones. At lower temperatures: Fe₂O₃ → Fe₃O₄ → FeO by CO. At higher temperatures: FeO + C → Fe + CO.
concepts.formula_reaction: 3Fe₂O₃ + CO → 2Fe₃O₄ + CO₂; Fe₃O₄ + 4CO → 3Fe + 4CO₂; FeO + C → Fe + CO

concepts.name: Extraction of Copper – Smelting
concepts.explanation: Copper is extracted from copper pyrites (CuFeS₂). The ore is roasted to remove S and oxidise Fe and Cu. FeO is slagged with SiO₂ (FeSiO₃). Cu₂S and FeS form copper matte. The matte is heated in a converter: 2Cu₂S + 3O₂ → 2Cu₂O + 2SO₂; 2Cu₂O + Cu₂S → 6Cu + SO₂. The product is blister copper.
concepts.formula_reaction: 2CuFeS₂ + O₂ → Cu₂S + 2FeS + SO₂; FeO + SiO₂ → FeSiO₃; 2Cu₂O + Cu₂S → 6Cu + SO₂

concepts.name: Extraction of Zinc
concepts.explanation: Zinc is extracted from zinc blende (ZnS). Roasting gives ZnO: 2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂. ZnO is reduced with coke: ZnO + C → Zn + CO. The metal is distilled off and collected.

concepts.name: Electrochemical Principles in Metallurgy
concepts.explanation: Reduction can also be achieved electrochemically. ΔG° = -nFE°. More reactive metals have large negative E° values, making their reduction difficult. The difference in E° values determines the feasibility of displacement reactions.
prerequisites: ELC_08 (Relationship between E°cell, ΔrG°, and K)

concepts.name: Extraction of Aluminium – Hall-Heroult Process
concepts.explanation: Pure Al₂O₃ is mixed with cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) to lower the melting point and increase conductivity. Electrolysis is carried out with graphite anode (which is consumed) and steel cathode. Overall: 2Al₂O₃ + 3C → 4Al + 3CO₂.
concepts.formula_reaction: Cathode: Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al; Anode: C + O²⁻ → CO + 2e⁻

concepts.name: Hydrometallurgy of Copper
concepts.explanation: Low-grade copper ores are leached with acid or bacteria. Cu²⁺ ions are reduced using scrap iron or H₂: Cu²⁺ + Fe → Cu + Fe²⁺; Cu²⁺ + H₂ → Cu + 2H⁺. Iron is preferred over zinc (cheaper, although zinc is more reactive).

concepts.name: Oxidation in Extraction – Chlorine from Brine
concepts.explanation: Chlorine is extracted by the oxidation of Cl⁻ from brine: 2Cl⁻ + 2H₂O → 2OH⁻ + H₂ + Cl₂. ΔG° = +422 kJ (E° = -2.2 V), so an external voltage >2.2 V is required. Electrolysis gives Cl₂, H₂, and NaOH.

concepts.name: Extraction of Gold and Silver by Leaching
concepts.explanation: Gold and silver are leached with dilute NaCN or KCN in the presence of air: 4M + 8CN⁻ + 2H₂O + O₂ → 4[M(CN)₂]⁻ + 4OH⁻. The metal is recovered by displacement with zinc: 2[Au(CN)₂]⁻ + Zn → 2Au + [Zn(CN)₄]²⁻.

concepts.name: Refining of Metals – Distillation and Liquation
concepts.explanation: Distillation: used for low-boiling metals like Zn and Hg. The impure metal is evaporated and condensed as pure metal. Liquation: used for low-melting metals like Sn. The metal flows away from higher melting impurities.

concepts.name: Refining of Metals – Electrolytic Refining
concepts.explanation: The impure metal is made the anode; a pure metal strip is the cathode. Electrolysis transfers pure metal from anode to cathode. Impurities less reactive than the metal form anode mud (e.g., Ag, Au, Pt in copper refining).
concepts.formula_reaction: Anode: M → Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻; Cathode: Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻ → M

concepts.name: Refining of Metals – Zone Refining
concepts.explanation: Based on the principle that impurities are more soluble in the melt than in the solid state. A circular heater moves along a rod of impure metal, creating a molten zone. Impurities move with the molten zone, concentrating at one end, which is cut off. Used for high-purity semiconductors (Ge, Si, B, Ga, In).

concepts.name: Refining of Metals – Vapour Phase Refining
concepts.explanation: The metal is converted to a volatile compound, purified, and then decomposed to give pure metal. Requirements: the metal should form a volatile compound that is easily decomposable. Examples: (i) Mond process for Ni: Ni + 4CO → Ni(CO)₄ → Ni + 4CO; (ii) van Arkel method for Zr/Ti: Zr + 2I₂ → ZrI₄ → Zr + 2I₂.
concepts.formula_reaction: Ni + 4CO → Ni(CO)₄ (330-350K); Ni(CO)₄ → Ni + 4CO (450-470K); Zr + 2I₂ → ZrI₄ → Zr + 2I₂

concepts.name: Refining of Metals – Chromatographic Methods
concepts.explanation: Based on the principle that different components are differently adsorbed on an adsorbent. The mixture is passed through a column of adsorbent (e.g., Al₂O₃); components are adsorbed at different levels and later eluted. Used for purifying elements available in minute quantities.

concepts.name: Uses of Aluminium, Copper, Zinc, and Iron
concepts.explanation: Aluminium: foils, paints, conductors, alloys (light). Copper: electrical wires, pipes, alloys (brass with Zn, bronze with Sn, coinage alloy with Ni). Zinc: galvanising iron, batteries, alloys (brass, german silver), reducing agent. Iron: cast iron (stoves, pipes, toys), wrought iron (anchors, wires), steel (alloy steel for cables, tools, stainless steel).
